coredata.dc:title: Biomass CQDs derivate carbon as high-performance anode for K-ion battery
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Dengyang
Wang, Qing
Tan, Mingxiu
Wang, Shasha
Luo, Shaohua
Hou, Pengqing
Zhang, Yahui
Yan, Shengxue
Liu, Xin
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a multifunctional carbon nanomaterial have drawn great attention in the field of energy attribute to quantum size, various functional groups and unique physicochemical properties. In this work, the CQDs were prepared from soybean by a simple hydrothermal method, then further pyrolyzed into honeycomb carbon (HC), spherical pore carbon (SC) and bulk porous carbon (BC) with the assistance of templates, respectively. As the anode materials in potassium ion battery (PIBs), HC delivered a high specific capacity of 280.5 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 and a superior rate performance (136 mAh g−1 at 5 A g−1), ascribing to its abundant surface active sites of potassium ion storage and special structures conducive to potassium ion diffusion. This study provides an excellent anode for PIBs from a simple route to construct CQDs-derived carbon.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Potassium ion batteries
One-step hydrothermal
Anodes
Biomass

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CQDs and its derivative carbon
Preparation of HC, SC and PC

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern; (b) FT-IR spectra of CQDs.
(a) XPS survey spectra; (b, c, d) high-resolution of C1s, N1s and O1s spectra of CQDs, respectively.
The SEM, TEM and HRTEM images of HC (a, b, c), SC (d, e, f), BC (g, h, i), respectively.
(a) XRD patterns, (b) Raman spectra, (c) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, (d) FT-IR spectra.
(a) XPS survey spectra; high-resolution of C 1 s spectra: (b) HC, (c) SC, (d) BC; (e) high-resolution N 1 s spectra of HC; (f) the content of N species in three samples.
(a) Initial three CV curves at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1, (b) first charge/discharge curves at 0.1 A g−1, (c) cycle performance at 0.1 A g−1, (d) rate properties, (e) cycle performance at 1 A g−1.
(a) CV curves of HC under different scan rates, (b) log(i) and log(v) plots, (c) capacitive contribution of HC at 1 mV s−1, (d) EIS plots of electrodes after 5 cycles and equivalent circuit.

head.ce:title: Biomass CQDs derivate carbon as high-performance anode for K-ion battery

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Northeastern University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a multifunctional carbon nanomaterial have drawn great attention in the field of energy attribute to quantum size, various functional groups and unique physicochemical properties. In this work, the CQDs were prepared from soybean by a simple hydrothermal method, then further pyrolyzed into honeycomb carbon (HC), spherical pore carbon (SC) and bulk porous carbon (BC) with the assistance of templates, respectively. As the anode materials in potassium ion battery (PIBs), HC delivered a high specific capacity of 280.5 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 and a superior rate performance (136 mAh g−1 at 5 A g−1), ascribing to its abundant surface active sites of potassium ion storage and special structures conducive to potassium ion diffusion. This study provides an excellent anode for PIBs from a simple route to construct CQDs-derived carbon.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: In recently years, high-efficiency energy storage devices and new energy storage technology have attracted wide attention [1,2]. Lithium-ion battery (LIBs) is the most widely used energy storage device. However, the relatively scarce of lithium resource (0.0017 wt%) has limited the development of LIBs in future energy storage market. Currently, the demand for future energy-storage systems with a high performance is more urgent than ever before [3–5]. PIBs are regarded as a complementary device with great potential for LIBs owing to the abundance (1.5 wt%), inexpensive and negative redox potential (−2.93 V) of K resources [6–9].

The practical initial coulombic efficiency, rate capability of metal-ion batteries largely determined by their electrode materials [10]. Hitherto, a wide variety of electrode materials, which involve conductive polymers, transition metal oxides, carbonaceous materials has been investigated [3,10–14]. Especially, carbonaceous materials have been acknowledged as one of the most powerful commercial electrode materials for PIBs because of their abundant nature sources, low price, chemical inertness and long life [7,15]. As an emerging member of carbon materials family, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with quantum size (less than 10 nm), unique optical properties and rich surficial groups have attracted great attention [16]. They have been widely applied in catalysis, biomedical, optoelectronic devices, etc[17–20]. Furthermore, CQDs also play a crucial part in exploring novel energy storage materials. CQDs possess significantly influence on promoting ion storage and transfer, due to high specific surface area, pendant functional groups, high chemical activity and nano size. CQDs are amorphous shell structure, usually composed of carbon core and abundant functional groups or polymer chains [21]. Therefore, a variety of CQDs-derived carbon materials with different morphological structures through a series of template-assisted, such as porous materials and nanofibers materials [22,23].

Currently, green chemical technology has become an important production process because of its clean, low-harm and sustainable development [24,25]. Preparation of CQDs using nontoxic and economical materials conforms to the concept of green chemistry. Therefore, renewable and eco-friendly biomass such as garlic, shrimp shell, lemon peel, pomelo peel, grass, and sweet potato used to produce CQDs have received great interest [17,26–29].

In this paper, the nitrogen self-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) was prepared from soybean by hydrothermal method. Furtherly, CQDs-derived hard carbon materials with honeycomb carbon (HC), spherical pore carbon (SC) and bulk porous carbon (BC) were synthesized using NaCl, SiO2 and ZnCl2 as template and utilized as anodes for PIBs. Among them, HC displays a high specific capacity of 280mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 and a remarkable rate performance (136 mAh g−1 at 5 A g−1), indicating its excellent cycle performance.

Soybean was bought from a supermarket. Sodium chloride and zinc chloride was supplied by Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. The SiO2 spheres ware obtained by laboratory self-made. Hydrofluoric acid and hydrochloric acid were received from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals without any further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs and its derivative carbon

ce:section.ce:para: The soybean was firstly grinded into powder, and then was added into H2O. Subsequently, poured into Teflon-lined autoclave with 180 ℃ for 3 h. The resulting product was filtered through a 450 nm membrane, and purified through a cellulose dialysis membrane bag (molecular weight cut-off: 1000 Da) for 2 days to remove excess impurities. Finally, CQDS powder obtained after freeze-drying for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of HC, SC and PC
ce:section.ce:para: 2.4 g templates (such as NaCl, SiO2 or ZnCl2) and 0.3 g of the as-prepared CQDs powders were mixed thoroughly by grinding, and then the mixtures heated at 800 ℃ for 2 h (5 ℃ min−1) under argon atmosphere. After natural cooling, the sample prepared by NaCl template was washed by deionized water to get honeycomb carbon (HC). The sample prepared by SiO2 template was placed in 20% HF and stirred for 12 h to obtain spherical pore carbon (SC). The sample prepared by ZnCl2 template was washed with 2 M HCl for 24 h, and then was further filtered with deionized water and absolute ethanol to obtain bulk porous carbon (BC). All the products were dried for 12 h at 60 °C.

The microstructure and morphologies were observed by Scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S-4800) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2F20). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were acquired from Rigaku Corporation diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation from 10˚ to 80˚. Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (FTIR-8400 s) and Raman spectroscopy (HORIBA Scientific Lab RAM HR Evolution) were used to observe the functional groups and Raman spectra. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, SCALAB 250 XI) was employed to analyze element of the materials. Surface area analyzer (Autosorb iQ Station 1) was detected BET specific surface area.

The product powders (80%), acetylene black (10%) and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) (10%) were uniformly mixed. Painting the homogenous slurry on Cu foil and dried for 8 h at 80 ℃. After that, the copper foil discs (diameter: 10 mm) dried again at 120 ℃ for overnight in a vacuum. Metallic potassium and the glass fiber was applied as counter electrode and separator, separately. The electrolyte consists of 0.8 M KFP6 in ethylene carbonate (EC) and diethyl carbonate (DEC) (1:1 vol%). Galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) and cycle performances test were completed by Neware test systems. CHI1000 equipment was applied to measure cyclic voltammograms (0.1–3 V, vs K/K+) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (100k to 0.01 Hz).

The microstructures of the obtained CQDs were performed by TEM. In Fig. 1a-b, the CQDs prepared by hydrothermal from soybean shows the shape of nanosphere with uniform particle size about 7–10 nm, implying its quantum dot size. As show in Fig. 2a, the XRD was applied to display the crystalline nature of CQDs. And at 2θ value of 20° observed a broad diffraction peak, revealing an amorphous carbon structure of CQDs [30]. The surface functional groups were exhibited by FTIR. As shown in Fig. 2b, the characteristic absorption bands at 3433 cm−1 ascribes to the stretching vibration of O-H/N-H groups. C-H stretching vibration related to two absorption bands at 2925 cm−1 and 2974 cm−1. The absorption bands at around 1631 cm−1 and 1772 cm−1 represent the CC and CO, respectively [31,32].

In addition, the C-N and C-O stretching vibration located at1465 cm−1 and 1089 cm−1 [30]. The FTIR results indicated the introduction of functional groups with hydroxyl, amidogen, carboxylic, and carbonyl on the surface of CQDs were attributed to the degradation of organics under hydrothermal treatment.

The further analysis of the surface chemical composition of as-obtained CQDs were record by XPS. In Fig. 3a, XPS survey spectrum shows that the CQDs contain carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements. As displayed in Fig. 3b, three peeks at 284.28ev, 286.6ev and 287.98ev divide from high-resolution spectrum of C1s respectively attributing to the C-C, CO and C-OH/C-N groups [33]. Fig. 3c shows the N1s spectrum of two peaks located at 399.24ev, 400.61ev, which can be assigned to C-N-C and N-H groups. The O1s spectrum in Fig. 3d exhibits three fitted peaks at 531.77, 532.85 and 533.92ev, confirming CO, C-O and C-OH/C-O-C groups, respectively [34,35]. The results of XPS spectrum consistent with FTIR, which further confirms that the CQDs might be some mixture of oligomers with different molecular weight [21].

By pyrolysis, the CQDs were converted into their derivative carbon materials. SEM patterns of different morphology of the as-obtained carbon materials are displayed in Fig. 4. When the CQDs were pyrolyzed, various functional groups were decomposed into carbon atoms and aggregated into cluster on the surface of templates through self-assembly. As displayed in Fig. 4a, after NaCl templates were washed by deionized water, carbonized product was converted into alveolate carbon structures carbon. In Fig. 4d, the CQDs were wrapped around the surface of SiO2 nanospheres and gradually carbonized with high temperature. After HF washing, SiO2 spheres were totally removed and the sample was transformed into spherical pore carbon materials. In Fig. 4g, different from the previous two templates, ZnCl2 templates change the CQDs into bulk porous structures carbon. During pyrolysis, many irregular pores and cracks on the surfaces of CQDs were formed, and the molten ZnCl2 occupied and connected pores and cracks. After the ZnCl2 removed, bulk porous carbon structures were formed. When the CQDs directly pyrolyzed without the introduction of template, the formed carbon exhibits an irregular flake block structure (Fig. S6), suggesting the role of template in controlling the formation of different morphologies carbon.

Further microstructures analysis of the carbon materials was conducted by TEM. As seen from Fig. 4b, e, h, the honeycomb structures of HC, spherical pore carbon of SC and bulk porous structures of BC can be obviously observed, respectively. Fig. 4c, f, i exhibited the HRTEM images of three samples. It can be observed that similar inner amorphous structures with disordered carbon phase of three samples, which indicates the properties of hard carbon.

XRD and Raman investigated the structure characteristics of three carbon materials. In Fig. 5a, XRD patterns display two broad peaks located at 24° and 44°, ascribing to (002) and (100) lattice planes, indicating that the as-prepared samples possess amorphous carbon structure [22,30]. From the Bragg equation, the interlayer spacing (d002) of HC (22.9°), SC (23.7°) and BC (25.1°) is calculated as 0.387 nm, 0.375 nm and 0.354 nm, respectively. Obviously, the interlayer spacing of three products are greater than graphite (0.335 nm), which boosts the insertion and desertion K+. Furthermore, the gentle peak at 44° further suggests the low degree of graphitization of the samples [30,36]. The XRD results is consistent with that of TEM.

To analyze the graphitization degree of products, Raman was implemented. Fig. 5b presents two distinct peaks at around 1331 cm−1 and 1578 cm−1, ascribed to the D-bond (disorder structure) and G-bond (graphite structure) [37]. The intensity ratio of ID/IG is a vital reference to compare disorder degree of carbon materials. The ID/IG value of HC, SC, BC are 1.15, 1.03 and 1.02, respectively. And HC obviously possesses a lower graphitization and more defects compared with SC and BC, thus improving the K-storage properties.

The pore distribution of three materials were presented by N2 adsorption-desorption technology. Fig. S1 shows the pore size distribution of HC, SC, and BC with scoping from 2 to 10 nm. The average pores diameter of HC, SC, and BC is 8.2, 3.1 and 4.2 nm, respectively. Moreover, in Fig. 5c, SC, HC and BC exhibit different specific surface area of 72.1, 242.6 and 785.5 m2 g−1, respectively. The different templates lead to the difference in surface area and pore distribution. Especially, in the preparation of carbon, ZnCl2 is not only a template, but also an activator, leading to larger specific surface area of BC than that of HC and SC.

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra confirm that as-obtained carbon materials contain plentiful functional groups in Fig. 5d, which are -OH, -C-N, -C-O, -CC and -CH3, respectively. Further investigation for the surface chemistry of specimens, the XPS spectra was measured. In Fig. 6a, the survey spectra present that.

three carbon materials possess the elements of C, N and O, and their detailed parameters of each sample are shown in Table S1 and all samples have similar C content. However, the BC has the least nitrogen content than HC and SC, which might ascribe to the more release of N element caused by the activation of ZnCl2. Fig. 6b displays the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of HC, the peaks at approximately 284.78, 285.7, 288.28 and 291.77 eV ascribed to C-C/CC, C-N, C-O and CO, respectively [30]. The C1s spectrum of SC and BC presenting the similar peaks and functional groups in Fig. 6c and d. In Figs. 6e and S2, the high-resolution of N 1 s spectra were displayed. In Fig. 6f, the peaks at around 397.58, 400.02 and 403.63 eV assigned to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively [8,38]. And three samples possess similar relative contents of different N species. The presence of three N species generates more defects and active sites, in the meantime, pyridinic N and pyrrolic N can promote the diffusion of K ions, and the conductivity of carbon materials improved by the presence of graphitic N.

To explore the electrochemical performances of as-obtained samples with potassium intercalation and deintercalation, Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was conducted. Figs. 7a and S3 exhibit the initial three cycles of CV curves under 0.1 mV s−1 from 0.01 V to 3 V of HC, SC and BC, respectively. In the first cycle, a board peak at around 0.6 V, vanishing in the following cycles. This result indicates the side reaction of electrolyte decomposition and the surface of the as-obtained electrode formation of SEI films. The sharp cathodic peaks appeared at 0.01 V, which relates to the intercalation of K+ into the KC8. In the subsequent sweep, weak humps between 0.2 V and 0.9 V. The sharp peak assigns to the behavior of K+ intercalating into graphite microcrystalline unit. The humps represent the surface-induced K+ storage. The anodic peak near 0.40 V reflect the deintercalation of K+. The CV curves of the subsequent two cycles almost overlap, suggesting the highly cyclic stability. Fig. 7b presents the first galvanostatic charge/discharge curves at 0.1 A g−1. The first discharge and charge capacities of HC, SC and BC are 874.5 and 280.5, 857.2 and 223.6 mAh g−1, 1839.6 and 219.7 mAh g−1, and their ICEs are 34.5%, 30.3%, and 22.3%, respectively. The low ICE is mainly resulted from the irreversible reaction and the formation of large number SEI films [21]. The cycling performance of carbon anodes at 0.1 A g−1 is presented in Fig. 7c. With the cycle number increase, the coulombic efficiency gradually reaches 100%. After 100 cycles, the capacity of SC, HC and PC maintains at 234.5, 190.1 and 153.9 mAh g−1, and their capacity retention ratio of 83.6%, 85.0% and 70.0%, respectively.

The rate performance of three electrodes with various current densities are shown in Fig. 7d. HC deliver specific capacity of 274.4, 231.8, 211.8, 193.0, 172.3 and 136.3mAh g−1 at current densities of 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2 and 5 A g−1, respectively. The specific capacity could remain at 241.3mAh g−1 when the current density returns to 0.1 A g−1. Accordingly, SC deliver specific capacity of 230.7, 185.6, 162.3, 145.9, 129.1 and 110.8 mAh g−1 and BC deliver specific capacity of 160.7, 119.8, 86.8, 64.2, 44.1 and 22.9 mAh g−1, respectively. When the current density returns to 0.1 A g−1, the specific capacity could recover to 186.4 mAh g−1 and 130.2 mAh g−1 respectively, when the current density returns to 0.1 A g−1. The results indicate that the HC presents superior rate capacity. Fig. 7e presents the long cycle performance of samples, and HC obtains 140.7mAh g−1 at 1 A g−1 after 1000cycles with a capacity retention ratio of 75.4%, suggesting its superb cyclic stability under high current density. Furthermore, the capacity of the carbon without template can remain at 78.6 mAh g−1 at a current density of 0.1 A g−1 after 100 cycles (Fig.S7a). The carbon without template delivers specific capacity of 64.2, 55.4, 27.6, 12.5 and 2.4 mAh g−1 at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2 and 5 A g−1, respectively. When the current density is returned to 0.1 A g−1, the capacity recovers to 71.5 mAh g−1 (Fig.S7b). And the initial capacity only is 9.6 mAh g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 (Fig.S7c).

To investigate more specifically kinetic properties involved in the three carbon electrodes, in Figs. 8a and S4 a, b, the CV curves of HC, SC and BC are measured at different scanning rates from 0.1 to 1 mV s−1. The capacity contributions of the three electrodes can be analyzed by the following Eqs. (1) and (2) [37,39]:
(1)
i = av b
(2) log(i) = log(a) + blog(v)

where peak current (i) and scan rate (v), a is a parameter, and the value of b can be calculated by a curve plotted by log(i)-log(v). Especially, b-value approaching 0.5, means a mainly process of diffusion-controlled K+ intercalation; while b-value is approximately to 1, indicates a mainly surface-induced capacitance process [39,40]. In Fig. 8b, the log(i)-log(v) curves of three specimens are plotted according to the CV curves of different sweeps and the b-value of HC, SC and BC is calculated to be 0.98, 0.95 and 0.94, respectively, implying that the capacity is mainly contributed by surface-capacitance behavior. In addition, the mixed mechanism of pseudocapacitive contribution can be quantitatively separated by Eq. (3) [31]:
(3)
i(V) = k1 v + k2 v 1/2

in which k1 v and k2 v 1/2 are associated with surface-capacitance and diffusion-controlled, respectively. The slope and intercept of the i(V)/v 1/2-v 1/2 fitted line could calculate the value of k1 and k2. Figs. 8c and S4c, d, display the profiles at a scan rate of 1 mV s−1, where the inner dark area indexed to the surface-capacitance contribution. The surface-capacitance contribution of HC, SC and BC at 1 mV s−1 is 89.1%, 84.4% and 72.3%, respectively. The pseudocapacitive contribution gradually increase with the increment of scan rates in Fig. S5. Furtherly, the HC always exhibits the most surface-induced capacity than SC and BC.

From the previous analysis, HC has more nitrogen active sites than SC and BC. Moreover, the honeycomb structures and lager specific surface area can provide K+ diffusion channels and K+ adsorption sites. Therefore, HC owns the excellent capacities and superior rate performance. Although BC has the highest specific surface area contributed by micropores among three carbon anodes, its average pore diameter is smaller than HC. In charge/discharge process, solvated K+ cannot transfer into some mismatched pores, resulting in small specific capacity of BC.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) spectra patterns matching equivalent circuits of three anodes after 5 cycles are shown in Fig. 8d. The Nyquist plots are composed of a large semicircle in the middle-high frequency region, standing for the charge transfer impedance, and an oblique line in the low frequency region, representing the Warburg impedance for the K+ diffusion. From the Fig. 8d, HC has lower charge-transfer and K+ diffusion resistance, indicating the honeycomb structures might be more conducive to the K+ transferring.

In summary, the biomass-derived CQDs and its derivative carbon with three morphologies were prepared. The CQDs present uniform nanospheres of 7–10 nm and contain surface functional groups such as hydroxyl, amidogen, carboxylic, and carbonyl. Through template technology, the CQDs-derived amorphous carbon with honeycomb (HC), spherical pore (SC) and bulk porous structures (BC) was prepared and applied as anodes for K-ion battery. HC exhibited an excellent specific capacity of 280.5 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 and a superior rate performance of 136 mAh g−1 at 5 A g−1. The results can be ascribed to reasonable K+ transmission pores structures and surface active sites of HC.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51874079, No. 51804035 and 11775226), the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (Nos. E2018501091, E2020501001, E2021501029 and A2021501007), the Science and Technology Project of Hebei Education Department (ZD2022158), the Hebei Province Key Research and Development Plan Project (No.19211302D), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. N2023040 and N2123035), the Central Guided Local Science and Technology Development Fund Project of Hebei Province (226Z4401G), S&T Program of Hebei (No. 22567627H).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2022.166260.
